title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
author: Guest Writer
orientation: Justin Weidman, a post-abortive father and signer of the Norman Statement, argues that newcomers to abolitionism who say 'I've always been an abolitionist; I just didn't know it' are dodging genuine repentance. He insists that real abolitionism requires confessing past apathy or prior pro-life incrementalism as sin and bearing fruit through active engagement, uncompromising advocacy, and calling others to repent.
audiences: Newcomer to abolitionism rebranding their past

questions.question: What is wrong with saying, 'I've always been an abolitionist; I just didn't know it'?
questions.audience: Newcomer to abolitionism rebranding their past
questions.answer: The phrase functions as a shortcut to avoid confessing prior indifference toward the killing of the preborn. It reframes past apathy as innocent ignorance rather than naming it as sin, which short-circuits the genuine repentance Scripture demands.
questions.quote: It often becomes a way to avoid the deep, uncomfortable burden that true repentance requires. Rather than confronting and confessing their prior indifference and neglect toward the immense suffering and dire plight of the preborn, many try to reinterpret or downplay their previous lack of awareness.

questions.question: How does the author distinguish 'course correction' from true repentance?
questions.audience: Newcomer to abolitionism rebranding their past
questions.answer: Course correction is presented as a self-soothing reframe of the past that updates one's language without confessing wrongdoing. True repentance requires acknowledging the past as sinful, seeking forgiveness, and turning from apathy into action.
questions.quote: Without repentance, course correction becomes a way to feel better about ourselves without actually doing what’s right.

questions.question: On what theological basis does the article condemn abortion?
questions.answer: The author appeals to Proverbs 6 to classify abortion as the shedding of innocent blood, an act God explicitly hates and calls an abomination. This frames the issue as a matter of God's revealed moral law, not policy preference.
questions.quote: “There are six things that the Lord hates, seven that are an abomination to him: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood.” — [Proverbs 6:16-17](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Proverbs%206%3A16-17&version=ESV) (ESV)

questions.question: What does the author say about Christians who supported pro-life incremental legislation in the past?
questions.answer: He treats prior support for incrementalism, or opting out of the fight altogether, as a hidden compromise that must be repented of. Apathy in the face of child sacrifice is named as guilt, not neutrality.
questions.quote: Many who claim to stand with abolitionists today are hiding a secret: They have previously supported incremental pro-life legislation or, worse, have opted out entirely from the ongoing efforts to uphold the sanctity of life. This is an undeniable fact, and such a stance is not a positive one.

questions.question: What replacement statement does the author suggest instead of 'I've always been an abolitionist'?
questions.audience: Newcomer to abolitionism rebranding their past
questions.answer: He offers a confessional script that names past indifference, credits God with opening one's eyes, and commits to active fighting for immediate abolition. The point is to put the language of repentance and resolve in place of self-flattering revisionism.
questions.quote: Rather than saying, *“I have always been an abolitionist,”* we should say with integrity, *“I have, in the past, been indifferent and apathetic toward the murder of children, but at long last God has opened my eyes to the reality surrounding me. I’ve kept my voice and mind quiet too long but no more! I will act, I will fight, I will seek to glorify God through the immediate and uncompromising abolition of abortion!”*

questions.question: What concrete actions count as 'fruit in keeping with repentance' for an abolitionist?
questions.answer: The author lists tangible activities: public evangelism, mobilizing the church, supporting abolition legislation, joining local abolition groups, sidewalk counseling, and lobbying for equal protection. Mere mental agreement does not qualify; activity is the proof.
questions.quote: **Real abolitionism is about activity.** If we have repented for our inactivity in the past, we must manifest that repentance through activity in the cause. **Whatever it takes**—engagement with abolition groups in your community, sidewalk counseling, or legislative lobbying for equal justice and equal protection—we must demonstrate our faith and obedience to God through concrete actions.

questions.question: Why does the author reject incremental anti-abortion legislation outright?
questions.answer: He argues incrementalism is itself unjust because it restricts abortion without criminalizing the murder of children, and delayed justice is denied justice. Repentance therefore includes refusing legislation that stops short of full abolition.
questions.quote: We must reject any exceptions, incrementalism, and partiality in abortion, because delayed justice is denied justice. Many who have long considered themselves part of the pro-life movement will come to realize that incremental legislation is inherently unjust.

questions.question: What responsibility does a repentant abolitionist have toward other Christians and church leaders?
questions.answer: The article calls believers to invite, and at times confront, other Christians who remain passive or accommodating, and to hold pastors accountable for preaching specifically against abortion. Calling others to repentance is treated as part of bearing fruit, not optional condemnation.
questions.quote: It means holding church leaders accountable for preaching not just about the sin of abortion, but specifically against it. It also means urging fellow believers to move beyond mere anti-abortion sentiment and become actively involved in the abolition of abortion.

questions.question: What hope does the article extend to those carrying the guilt of past abortion or apathy?
questions.answer: The author insists God forgives even those who have aborted their own children when they come humbly through Christ, removing shame and guilt by His Son. The remaining shame, he argues, belongs not to past sin confessed, but to refusing to change once the truth is known.
questions.quote: There is forgiveness even for those who have aborted their own children and now bear shame. God eliminates their shame and guilt by bringing peace by repentance through his Son. But we must openly admit that we have been indifferent and apathetic toward image bearers.

questions.question: How does the author frame the personal decision facing each reader at the end of the piece?
questions.audience: Newcomer to abolitionism rebranding their past
questions.answer: He places the choice on each reader's conscience but raises the stakes by invoking Luke 12:48: those given knowledge of abortion's reality will be held to a higher standard. The expected response is action grounded in repentance and submission to Christ as King.
questions.quote: Will you make that first move in committing yourself to abolition? That is a matter resting in your conscience alone. But remember: To whom much is given, much will surely be demanded (Luke 12:48). In the present, we must bear fruit that reflects true repentance.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Directly parallel argument that repentance must be modeled and demanded within the abolitionist movement itself.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Reinforces the article's claim that incremental pro-life legislation is incompatible with the repentance abolitionism requires.

related.title: My Pro-Life-to-Abolitionist Conversion
related.reason: First-person testimony of the very 'pro-life to abolitionist' transition Weidman calls readers to undertake with genuine confession.

related.title: Timothy Cole: My Conversion From Pro-Life Speaker to Abolitionist
related.reason: Another conversion narrative illustrating what naming past pro-life involvement as wrong, then changing course, looks like in practice.

related.title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers
related.reason: Same site, same Guest Writer byline, shared categories on abolitionism and repentance; complements the call to active, uncompromising abolition.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Theological backing for the article's rejection of incremental legislation as inherently unjust before a holy God.

related.title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Companion exhortation from the same site and Guest Writer on the spiritual posture required of an active abolitionist.
